text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Privacy, Confidentiality, and Public Records lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI use in public health must account for PHI, PII, confidential public health records, investigation data, data-sharing limits, retention rules, and public records obligations.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI use in public health must account for PHI, PII, confidential public health records, investigation data, data-sharing limits, retention rules, and public records obligations.

text: Privacy, confidentiality, and records controls are foundational for STLT public health AI because departments handle sensitive information, regulatory records, investigations, partner data, and public accountability obligations.
textPreview: Privacy, confidentiality, and records controls are foundational for STLT public health AI because departments handle sensitive informatio...

text: Sensitive or confidential data can be exposed, retained, reused, or disclosed if staff use the wrong AI environment.
textPreview: Sensitive or confidential data can be exposed, retained, reused, or disclosed if staff use the wrong AI environment.

text:
Use this slide in the Privacy, Confidentiality, and Public Records training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 7, 10, 15, 17, 34.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Sensitive or confidential data can be exposed, retained, reused, or disclosed if staff use the wrong AI environment.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Privacy, Confidentiality, and Public Records in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Privacy, Confidentiality, and Public Records training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 7, 10, 15, 17, 34.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI can change where data travel, who can access them, how long they are retained, and whether outputs become records. Staff need plain-language training on what information may be used, which AI environments are approved, and when legal, privacy, records, or data-sharing review is required.

Public information that can safely be used in approved public-facing contexts. Internal information that may be used in enterprise tools under agency policy. Confidential, identifiable, or protected information that requires restricted.

text: AI prompts, outputs, summaries, edits, decisions, and approvals may become records depending on jurisdiction and use. Departments should define when AI-assisted work must be retained, where it is stored, and how it is labeled. Staff should.

text: Classify data before selecting an AI tool. Review data use agreements, vendor terms, retention rules, and public records obligations. Train staff on prohibited data entry and escalation pathways for uncertain cases.

Start with Key Data Categories, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.

text:
Use this slide in the Privacy, Confidentiality, and Public Records training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 7, 10, 15, 17, 34.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

Which types of department data should never be entered into public AI tools? Where should AI prompts and outputs be stored when they support official work? Who decides whether a proposed AI use requires privacy, legal, or records review?

text: Sort example AI tasks into public, internal, confidential, and highly restricted data-use categories, then identify the approved AI environment and review pathway for each.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI data classification examples, Privacy escalation rules, Public records documentation notes.
Use this topic with Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 7, 10, 15, 17, 34. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.
